books.title: The Curriculum Vitae
books.subtitle: A complete, worked example of a private-service CV, and the discipline behind every line of it.
books.intro:
Everything for your CV is in one place. This reference explains how to build a private-service CV, and the document below contains three parts: the instructions, a finished example to aim for, and a blank template with the same structure that you fill in with your own life. Use the button above to download it as a PDF.

Prefer to edit it directly? Download the editable Word version.

sections.html:
The person who reads your CV is not a recruiter in the ordinary sense. It is a principal, a family office, or a consultant who has read four hundred of these. They will give yours somewhere between forty and ninety seconds, and in that time they are answering one question: can this person be trusted with a life?

A private CV is therefore not a career history. It is a demonstration of judgement, and the first thing it demonstrates is what you chose to leave out. Restraint is the very first quality this world looks for, and your CV is the first place it will find it, or fail to. One page. Never two.

There are two schools, and you should know both. In private household service, especially through European agencies and for live-in or family-facing roles, a discreet, professional headshot is common and often expected. It reassures, it personalises, and its absence can read as a small omission. This is why the example here includes one.

In other contexts, chiefly direct applications in the United States, a photo is discouraged and occasionally a liability, because of anti-discrimination norms in hiring. The rule, then, is not "always" or "never", it is "read the destination". Include the photo when you apply through an agency or to a European household. Consider omitting it for a direct US application, or keep a second, photo-free version ready.

When you do include one: a real professional headshot, shoulders up, plain background, warm but composed, dressed as you would for the interview. No holiday crop, no filter, no selfie. The photo should say discretion and standard, exactly as the rest of the page does.

sections.h: The golden rule: never a name
sections.html:
Never write the name of a family, a principal, a residence, a yacht or a company you served privately. A CV that names a former private employer will be discarded by anyone serious, and rightly so, because it tells them precisely how yours will be handled next.

Instead, describe the shape of the role. "A UHNW family (private), principal residences in London and Gstaad." "A single-family office, residences in Monaco and Los Angeles." "A private luxury villa, seasonal, on the Cote d'Azur." This protects the family, and it proves the discretion you are claiming, in the same line.

Amateurs list tasks. Professionals state scope, and let numbers do the only boasting that is permitted here.

Not "managed calendars and travel". Instead: "ran the personal affairs of a family across three residences, coordinating twenty vendors and a household budget of 1.2M." Numbers, staff led, residences run, budget held, guests hosted, savings found, are the quiet proof of the size of the world you can hold. Use them, and use nothing louder.

Header. Your name, then one line beneath it: the role you are for, not only the role you had. "Private House and Estate Manager." Then a compact strip of essentials: location, mobility, languages, right to work, licences.

The line (tagline). One sentence, in italics, directly under the header. It is the only place you are permitted to speak of yourself in the round. Keep it to a breath: years of service, the kind of houses, and the discretion they required.

Profile. Three or four sentences. Who you are in a house, what you are known for, and the reassurance a principal is looking for. Calm, anticipatory, exact.

Experience. Reverse chronological. For each role: the kind of household (never the name), your title, the dates, then two to four lines. Each line starts with a verb and ends with a consequence.

Core competencies. A short, scannable block: household and estate operations, staff leadership, budgets and vendors, private travel and aviation, entertaining, security liaison, inventory, discretion. Only what you could defend, calmly, in an interview.

Languages and mobility. Stated plainly, with honest levels. In this world, mobility is not a detail, it is a qualification. If you will relocate, say so.

Education and training. Your certifications, your academy, first aid, food hygiene, driving. Relevant, not exhaustive.

References. One line: "Available on request, subject to the consent of the household." That sentence alone tells them you understand the whole profession.

No home address, no date of birth, no marital status, no photograph of anything but your face, no salary history, no reference to a named principal, and no design that draws attention to itself. Leave out every adjective you cannot prove and every word a serious person would find loud: "iconic", "passionate", "world-class". The confidence is in the restraint.

Cream or white, ink, one accent used sparingly, one clean serif and one clean sans. Generous margins. Nothing decorative. The document should feel like a calling card left on a silver tray, not a portfolio. If a choice makes it look more designed, it is the wrong choice.

sections.h: The worked example, and why it works
sections.html:
The downloadable example belongs to Camille Laurent, an imagined Private House and Estate Manager with twelve years behind her. Read it against everything above and you will see the discipline in action: every private employer is described by shape, never by name; every role leads with scope and closes on a number; the photograph is discreet and professional; the competencies are all defensible; and the references line does the quiet, knowing work it is meant to.

It is not a real person, and that is the point. It is the standard, cleaned of any single life, so that you can build your own to meet it.

One page, and it breathes. No name of any private employer, anywhere. Every experience line begins with a verb and ends with a consequence. At least one honest number in each senior role. Photograph chosen for the destination, and professional if included. Languages and mobility stated. References line present. Read aloud once, slowly, for the single typo that undoes all of it. Then, and only then, send.

books.title: The Household Operations Toolkit
books.subtitle: The real working documents of the private-service profession, ready to use, ready to carry into your first house.
books.intro: A confidential resource of Maison Amarine. These are the instruments a professional runs a household with. Learn them here, adapt them to your house, and you will look like someone who has done this for years, from your very first week.

sections.html:
A great house is not run on memory or charm. It is run on quiet systems that make excellence repeatable and make you, over time, impossible to replace. The tools in this kit are those systems.

Two rules govern all of them. First, discretion: every one of these documents contains private information, so it is kept secure, never left open, never photographed, never taken home, and never discussed. Second, the manual survives you: you build these so that if you were gone tomorrow, the house would keep running. Paradoxically, that is exactly what makes a household refuse to let you go.

Use each template below as a skeleton. Fill it, tailor it to your principal, and keep it current. A tool that is out of date is worse than no tool at all.

What it is. The single reference that describes how the house runs: its people, its standards, its rhythms, its suppliers, its emergencies. If a new member of staff arrived tomorrow, this is what would make them useful in a day rather than a month.

What it contains.

  • The house. Addresses, access, alarm and gate procedures, room by room notes, systems (heating, water, pool, generator), and who services each one.
  • The people. The family and their key contacts; the staff, roles and hours; the reporting lines. Who decides what, and who is merely relayed to.
  • The standards. How the house is presented when the principal is in, and when guests are expected. Table settings, flowers, linens, temperatures, lighting, scent. The specifics that make a house feel cared for.
  • The rhythms. The daily, weekly, monthly and seasonal tasks. Deep cleans, window cleaning, garden cycles, wardrobe changeovers, wine deliveries.
  • The emergencies. A cross-reference to the Emergency Protocol (tool 6).

How to use it. Start it in your first fortnight, quietly, by observing. Do not present it. Build it for the house's resilience and your own value, and update it the day anything changes.

What it is. The most valuable and most confidential document you will ever keep: the record of your principal's preferences, so that their world is arranged to their taste without their ever having to ask. Old service families simply call it the book.

What it records (a preference, never an opinion, and never a judgement):

  • The table. Foods loved and refused, allergies, how coffee and tea are taken, still water not sparkling, the wines kept and the ones never served, portion sizes, timings.
  • Comfort. Room temperature, the side of the bed, pillow firmness, the blanket on a flight, the scent of the house, morning light or drawn curtains.
  • Dress and travel. Preferred airlines and seats, hotels and suites returned to, the car and the route, packing preferences, the toiletries always replaced.
  • People and dates. Names of family, godchildren, key staff and friends; anniversaries and birthdays; the flowers a spouse loves; the charity that matters.
  • The unspoken. What is never to be discussed, who is never to be put through, the subjects to steer away from.

How to use it. Build it from a thousand small observations, not from asking. When you notice a preference, record it that evening and act on it the next morning. This single document is the difference between service that reacts and service that anticipates.

What it is. The schedule of who is on duty, when, and where, including cover, rotations and time off. A well-kept rota is invisible; a badly kept one is felt by everyone in the house.

What it holds.

  • Each person, their role, their contracted hours and their days off.
  • The weekly grid: mornings, afternoons, evenings, and any night cover.
  • Rotations across residences, and travel schedules that follow the principal.
  • Cover for illness and holidays, agreed in advance, never improvised.
  • A clear note of who holds keys, codes and responsibilities on any given shift.

How to use it. Publish it in advance so the team can plan their lives; defend the days off you have promised (the martyr who gives up their rest is on the list of failures for a reason); and never build a rota that only works if nobody is ever ill.

A working, editable version of the rota is provided in the companion spreadsheet.

What it is. The financial record of the household: what is spent, on what, and against which budget. Handling it cleanly is the fastest way to become indispensable, and mishandling it the fastest way to leave.

What it tracks.

  • The annual and monthly budget by category: staff, provisions, maintenance, vendors, travel, entertaining, discretionary.
  • Every expense, dated, categorised, with the receipt kept.
  • Petty cash and any float held, reconciled to the pound.
  • A monthly summary the principal or the family office can read at a glance, delivered without being asked.

The golden rule. Report before you are asked, and never let the principal discover a number you should have surfaced. Precision here buys you trust everywhere else.

A working, editable budget and expense tracker is provided in the companion spreadsheet.

What it is. The single document that makes a principal's journey seamless, from the door of one home to the door of the next. You prepare one for every trip.

What it contains.

  • The itinerary. Every movement with times, in the principal's time zone and local time: cars, aviation, transfers, arrivals.
  • Aviation. Aircraft, tail number, FBO, crew contact, catering, luggage limits, ground handling.
  • The stay. Hotel or villa, suite, check-in arrangements, the room readied to preference (tool 2), a local contact on the ground.
  • The book on the move. Restaurants reserved, appointments, security coordination, the pharmacy and hospital nearest to each location.
  • Contingencies. The plan if a flight slips, a weather alternative, a spare of everything that matters.

How to use it. Build it days ahead, never discovered on the day. Anticipate jet lag by quietly adjusting the first day's plans and meals. Give the principal a life that simply flows, and let the work be invisible.

What it is. The calm, written answer to the question no one wants to face in the moment. In a crisis you do not think, you follow the protocol, and that is precisely why it exists.

What it covers.

  • Medical. Each family member's allergies, conditions, medications and blood type; the nearest hospital and a trusted private doctor for every residence; the location of first-aid and any prescribed devices.
  • Security. The close-protection team and how to reach them; never plan a movement without them; the procedure for an unknown person at the gate or a journalist's approach; safe rooms and evacuation routes.
  • The house. Fire, flood, power failure and alarm procedures; the shut-off points for water, gas and electricity; the engineers on call.
  • Escalation. Who is called, in what order, for what kind of event. The one page you could hand to a stranger and have them act correctly.

How to use it. Write it before you need it, keep it current, and make sure the whole team knows where it lives. Rehearse the fire and evacuation steps quietly, once, so they are muscle memory.

What it is. Your private, confidential register of trusted contacts, built one favour at a time over a career: the people who answer, who deliver, who never let you down. It is the quiet reason a principal keeps you, and it is never lent.

What it holds (per contact: name, what they do, why they are trusted, how to reach them, and any account or terms):

  • The florist who answers at midnight, the private chef available at short notice, the wine merchant, the tailor and the shoe repair.
  • The villa and yacht agents, the charter broker, the fixer in each city the family visits.
  • The doctor, the dentist, the vet, the discreet clinic.
  • The car service, the private aviation contact, the security firm.
  • The restaurants that will find a table, the maitres d' who know your principal by name.

How to use it. Guard it as you would a confidence. Build it deliberately: every excellent supplier you meet goes in, with a note on why. In five years it is worth more than any single skill you own.

What it is. The record of what the house holds: the fine and the fragile, the valuable and the insured. It protects the principal, and it protects you.

What it lists.

  • Art, antiques, silver, fine china and glass, with provenance where it exists (tool for a future conversation with a collector).
  • Wine and spirits, logged by the cellar record.
  • Watches, jewellery and other high-value items, with their locations and insured values.
  • Vehicles, and any registered assets of the residence.
  • Condition notes and photographs, and the date of the last check.

How to use it. Keep it current, reconcile it periodically, and never let a valuable move in or out of the house without a record. When something is lent, insured, restored or sold, the register is updated the same day.

sections.h: The one page that matters most
sections.html:
If you build only one of these in your first month, build the Principal's Book. Everything else runs the house; the book is what makes the principal feel known. And in this profession, to be trusted with a life, and to arrange it so gracefully that the person never has to ask, is the whole of the art.

Maison Amarine · The Private Concierge Academy · Confidential, for the use of our mentees only.

books.title: The Masterclass Series
books.subtitle: Six advanced modules for the specialist worlds a private concierge must move through with ease.
books.intro: The eight-week programme makes you fluent in the craft. These masterclasses make you fluent in its specialist domains, the ones where a single wrong word marks you as an outsider and a single right one marks you as a professional. Read the one you need before you need it.

sections.html:
Who is who. The captain holds absolute authority aboard, in law, over the vessel and everyone on it, including the principal's requests. You never instruct a captain; you ask what is possible. The chief stewardess runs the interior and the guest experience, and is your true counterpart on the water. The charter broker represents the principal; the central agent represents the yacht. A yacht manager runs the vessel's operations year-round, and on larger yachts a purser manages the accounts and the APA.

The vocabulary that matters. The charter fee is the base price to hire the yacht. On top sit the APA (Advance Provisioning Allowance, usually 25 to 35 per cent of the fee, held by the captain for fuel, food, dockage and extras), VAT depending on cruising waters, and a crew gratuity of roughly 5 to 15 per cent. The contract is the MYBA agreement. Delivery and redelivery define where the charter legally begins and ends.

The number nobody quotes. A yacht advertised at 200,000 a week is never 200,000. Add APA, VAT and gratuity and the real, all-in figure is closer to 290,000. Always quote your principal the all-in cost, never the sticker price.

How you operate. You build the relationship with the chief stewardess first; the whole trip runs through it. And the sentence that shows you know this world: "Captain, the principal would like to be in Portofino for dinner on Thursday. What is possible?"

The three ways to fly private. Ownership (the family owns the aircraft, with a flight department or a management company). Fractional ownership (a share in an aircraft, bought as a set number of hours per year). Charter (hire by the trip). Each has different economics, and knowing which your principal uses tells you who to call.

The vocabulary. The FBO is the private terminal you arrive at, not the main airport; there is no queue and often no walk. The tail number identifies the aircraft. An empty leg is a repositioning flight with no passengers, offered at a fraction of the price. Ground handling covers everything on the tarmac. Catering, luggage limits and crew are all arranged ahead through the operator.

How you operate. You confirm the aircraft, the FBO, the crew contact and the catering days ahead. You pack to the aircraft's limits, not the family's optimism. You anticipate jet lag by quietly adjusting the first day's meals and plans. And you never let a principal discover a delay from anyone but you, with the alternative already in hand.

The register to know. Learn the difference between charter, fractional and empty leg, and you can offer real value on a principal's travel without ever compromising on privacy or standard.

Service. Silver service transfers food from platter to plate with a spoon and fork in one hand; where it is expected, competence is assumed and clumsiness is instantly seen. Know the brigade of a kitchen (chef de cuisine, sous-chef, chefs de partie) so you address the right person for the right thing. The maitre d'hotel runs the dining room, and a relationship with a great one opens tables no booking system can.

Wine. A cru is a vineyard of recognised quality; grand cru and premier cru are the highest classifications in regions like Burgundy and Bordeaux. Decanting separates an older red from its sediment and lets it breathe; offer it for a mature red without being asked. En primeur is buying wine as a future, still in the barrel. Trust the sommelier and ask their guidance rather than performing knowledge; it yields the finer pairing.

The map to carry. Know the strong and weak vintages of the major regions, recognise a grand cru on a list, and understand that serious Champagne is now often served in a white-wine glass, not a flute, to release its aroma. Know what a Michelin star truly means: one is a real achievement, three is exceptionally rare.

The one rule. Guide a hesitant principal gently toward the chef's judgement, the tasting menu, the sommelier's pairing. The confidence to defer is itself a mark of taste.

The word that governs everything. Provenance: the documented history of ownership of a work. Strong provenance authenticates, protects value and reassures; a gap in it raises questions. Always ask for it before advising a purchase, and never let a valuable move without a record (see the Inventory in your toolkit).

How the world works. An art advisor guides acquisitions and sales discreetly. Auction houses run on lots, reserves (the minimum the seller will accept) and a buyer's premium on top of the hammer price. A freeport is a secure, tax-advantaged storage facility where serious collections often live. Conservation and handling are specialist trades; fine art is moved by dedicated shippers, never by the household.

How you operate. You coordinate, you never handle. You keep the register current, you manage the specialists (shippers, conservators, insurers, hangers), and you protect the collection's paperwork as carefully as the works themselves. When something is lent, insured, restored or sold, the record is updated the same day.

The mark of the insider. Old money treasures patina, the evidence of age and quality; to over-restore an heirloom is to erase its story. Knowing this, and saying it at the right moment, signals real fluency.

Who they are. The close-protection team, or the detail, is the trained team around the principal and the family. You coordinate with them; you do not manage them, and you never plan a movement without them. The best relationship with a detail is quiet, respectful and constant.

The vocabulary. An advance is the reconnaissance of a location before the principal arrives. A threat assessment shapes how a trip is run. Comms, routes, timings, guest lists and schedules are shared with the team, always. Residential security covers the home; the detail covers the person.

How you operate. You share the itinerary early and completely. You treat every route, guest and movement as sensitive information. You know the procedure for an unknown person at the gate and for a journalist's approach: decline, and flag it. A beautiful itinerary that ignores security is not a service, it is a risk.

The reflex. Before you confirm any plan, ask yourself who on the detail needs to know, and tell them first. Anticipation, here, can matter more than anywhere else in the house.

What it is. The private structure that manages a wealthy family's wealth, investments, legal affairs, philanthropy and often its household staff. A single-family office serves one family; a multi-family office serves several. In many houses it is the family office, not the family, that actually hires you, pays you, and holds your reference.

Who is who. Know who signs, who decides, and who merely relays. The chief of staff or principal's private office sits at the top of the operation; the estate manager runs the residences; the family office runs the money and the structure. Confusing these lines is a classic and costly error.

How you operate. You learn the reporting lines in your first week and you respect them absolutely. Budgets, expenses and household reporting flow to the family office cleanly and on time (see the Budget in your toolkit). You treat the office with the same discretion you give the family, because they are the same confidence.

The professional's summit. The most senior role in this world, chief of staff to a private household, is reached through years of proven trust, never through a certificate. Understanding the family office is the first step in seeing the whole board, and one day, perhaps, running it.

Maison Amarine · The Private Concierge Academy · Confidential, for the use of our mentees only.

books.title: The Casebook
books.subtitle: Real situations from the private-service world, with the wrong moves, the professional's move, and the principle beneath each one.
books.intro: You will not be tested on what you can recite. You will be tested, in a real house, on what you do in the ten seconds after something goes wrong. This casebook trains exactly that. Read each case, decide your move before you read the answer, and let the principles become instinct.

sections.h: Case 1 · The wine on the Birkin
sections.html:
The situation. At a private dinner, a guest reaches across the table and knocks a full glass of red wine over the principal's cream Birkin, resting on the empty chair beside her. The table goes quiet. The guest is mortified. The principal glances at you.

The wrong moves. To gasp or react to the damage. To fuss over the bag at the table and prolong the moment. To reassure the guest loudly, which only deepens their embarrassment and draws more attention.

The professional's move. You are beside the principal within seconds, calm and unhurried. You lift the bag away discreetly, murmur that it will be taken care of, and immediately turn the room's attention back to the evening: a fresh glass, a quiet word, the service flowing again. The bag goes at once to a specialist leather cleaner from your black book; you never attempt to treat it yourself. The next morning you report to the principal what has been done, not what went wrong.

The principle. In a crisis at the table, the object is never the priority; the atmosphere is. You protect the evening first, the belonging second, and you make the accident disappear so smoothly that within a minute it is as if it never happened.

The situation. You discover that the principal has agreed to be in two places on Thursday, a board meeting in Geneva at 9am and a family lunch in London at 1pm, and only one aircraft is available. Neither commitment has been cancelled. Neither party knows about the other.

The wrong moves. To wait and hope it resolves itself. To cancel the one you personally judge less important. To present the clash to the principal as a problem, with no solution attached.

The professional's move. You confirm the hard constraints quietly (aircraft availability, flight times, ground transfers), then you build two or three viable options: a repositioning that makes both possible, a later lunch, a second aircraft chartered for the gap. You bring the clash and the solutions to the principal together, in one calm sentence: "There is an overlap on Thursday. Here are two ways it works, and my recommendation."

The principle. Never surface a problem without a path through it. Anticipation is catching the clash early; professionalism is arriving with the solution already in hand, so the principal only ever has to choose.

sections.h: Case 3 · The journalist at the gate
sections.html:
The situation. A polite, well-dressed person appears at the residence, says they are writing a warm profile of the family, and asks you to confirm just one small, harmless-sounding detail. They are friendly, and they are not going away.

The wrong moves. To confirm the "harmless" detail. To deny it (a denial is still information). To engage in conversation at all, however pleasant.

The professional's move. You are courteous and completely closed. You neither confirm nor deny anything, you offer nothing, and you end the exchange warmly but firmly. You then report the approach immediately to the family office and the security detail, with the time, the description and what was asked.

The principle. A journalist counts on a friendly slip. Discretion here is not silence about secrets, it is refusing to be a source at all, and treating even the smallest detail as sacred. What you must not do is the one thing that feels most natural: be helpful.

sections.h: Case 4 · The housekeeper and the chef
sections.html:
The situation. Two long-serving members of staff, the housekeeper and the private chef, are in open conflict. The tension is affecting the whole house. Each comes to you privately, certain they are right, and each expects you to take their side.

The wrong moves. To take a side to end it quickly. To ignore it and hope it passes. To carry each one's complaints to the other, which pours fuel on the fire.

The professional's move. You listen to each, fully and without judgement, and you commit to nothing in the moment. You address the practical friction (whose station is whose, how the kitchen and the house intersect, timings) rather than the personalities. You speak to them together only once you understand both sides, and you frame it as the smooth running of the house, not as a verdict on who is right.

The principle. Harmony among staff is itself a service to the principal. Your job is not to win the argument for someone, it is to remove the friction and restore the calm, while keeping the respect of both. Alliances built on taking sides always curdle.

sections.h: Case 5 · The outburst that is not about you
sections.html:
The situation. The principal snaps at you sharply over something small, a car that is early, a coffee a shade too cool, in a tone far beyond the moment. You have done nothing wrong.

The wrong moves. To defend yourself and explain. To take it personally and go cold. To match the emotion.

The professional's move. You absorb it calmly, fix the small thing without a word of defence, and read what is underneath. Very often the sharpness is anxiety about something ahead, and it has simply landed on you. You quietly ease whatever is really weighing on the day, and you let the moment pass without making them apologise for it.

The principle. Read the emotion beneath the words, and solve the feeling, not just the sentence. Taking it personally is the rookie error; the professional understands that they are, in that moment, the safest place for the principal to put a difficult feeling, and they carry it without flinching.

sections.h: Case 6 · The secret a colleague "already knows"
sections.html:
The situation. The principal told you something in confidence weeks ago. Now another member of staff hints, knowingly, that they know it too, and waits for you to confirm.

The wrong moves. To confirm it, since they "obviously already know". To ask them who told them. To look flustered, which confirms it anyway.

The professional's move. You neither confirm nor deny. You stay warm and unreadable, you hold exactly what you were told, and you let the hint fall flat. If the leak is significant, you note it, but you do not become the second source.

The principle. A confidence does not lapse because someone else guessed. "They already know" is the oldest trap in the house, and the professional never walks into it. What you were told in trust stays with you, whatever anyone else appears to know.

sections.h: Case 7 · The vendor who offers you a cut
sections.html:
The situation. A supplier you use for the household quietly offers you a personal commission to keep sending them the family's business, or you discover the household is being overcharged by a vendor connected to another member of staff.

The wrong moves. To accept the arrangement, "since everyone does it". To confront the vendor or the colleague dramatically. To say nothing and let it continue.

The professional's move. You decline any personal benefit, immediately and without ambiguity. Where the household is being overcharged, you gather the facts quietly, then raise it cleanly with the family office or principal, framed as protecting the household's interests, not as accusing a colleague. You let the structure handle the person.

The principle. Your integrity around the household's money is the fastest way to become indispensable, and the fastest way to lose everything if it slips. You never take a cut, and you protect the principal's purse as if it were a confidence, because it is one.

The situation. You dropped a ball. A booking missed, a valuable chipped, an instruction misread. Only you know, and it would be easy to say nothing.

The wrong moves. To hide it and hope. To take forty minutes explaining how it happened. To over-apologise until the principal has to reassure you.

The professional's move. You take four minutes, not forty: fact, fix, ownership. You state plainly what happened, you present the solution already underway, you own it without excuses, and you move on. You do not relitigate it, and you do not let it become a performance of guilt.

The principle. Trust survives an honest mistake; it rarely survives a hidden one. Hiding an error is the single failure that almost never survives discovery. Fact, fix, ownership, and then let it go, is the whole of professional recovery.

Do not read it once. Come back to a case the week before you start a role, and again whenever a real situation echoes one here. In time, the move will arrive before the thought does, and that is the point. A household does not keep the person who knows the theory. It keeps the person who, in the ten seconds after something goes wrong, simply does the right thing.

Maison Amarine · The Private Concierge Academy · Confidential, for the use of our mentees only.

books.title: Cultural Fluency
books.subtitle: The codes of service change with the family you serve. This is the map that keeps you from a quiet, unforgettable mistake.
books.intro: A note before anything else: what follows are patterns, not rules. Wealth is global and individual, and a family will always override the tendencies of the culture they come from. Read the person, never the flag. But knowing these patterns means you arrive prepared, you avoid the error that is never mentioned and never forgotten, and you show, without a word, that you understand the world you are entering.

sections.html: Before you serve any family, do three things. Learn, discreetly, where they are from and how they live. Observe, in your first days, more than you act. And when in doubt, ask a trusted colleague or the family office quietly, rather than assume. Cultural fluency is not performing knowledge of a culture; it is the humility to notice, and the care to get the small things right.

The spirit. Hospitality is close to sacred. A guest is honoured generously, and the household is expected to reflect that same warmth and abundance. Generosity, dignity and respect for hierarchy run through everything.

What it means for your service.

  • Religion shapes the rhythm. Prayer times, Ramadan (when the household's hours and meals shift entirely, fasting by day and gathering by night), and the significance of Friday. Food is halal; alcohol, where present at all, is handled with discretion and never assumed.
  • Coffee and dates (gahwa) are a ritual of welcome, offered promptly and correctly. Learn the form.
  • Modesty and privacy, particularly around the women of the family, are treated with great care. Discretion about the household is absolute.
  • Time has a different texture. Plans can shift; patience and grace matter more than rigid punctuality, and generosity of spirit is read as respect.
  • Gifts and gestures carry weight; understand their meaning before you give or receive.

The reflex. Lead with warmth, respect the faith and its rhythms without ever being asked to, and treat the family's privacy as the highest duty.

The spirit. Warm informality laid over very high standards. The register is friendly and direct, but the expectation of excellence is total, and efficiency is prized.

What it means for your service.

  • Informality is a style, not a lowering of standards. You may be on first-name terms and spoken to as a valued professional, yet the bar for delivery is as high as anywhere.
  • Directness and speed. Clear communication, fast execution, solutions over process. A problem is expected to arrive with its answer.
  • Wellness and longevity are central: trainers, nutritionists, recovery, biohacking, the best doctors. This is a serious part of the modern American household.
  • Philanthropy is often woven into the family's identity and calendar; galas, foundations, causes.
  • Tipping and gratuity norms are their own culture; learn them per setting, because getting them wrong is quietly noticed.

The reflex. Be warm, be fast, be flawless. Match the easy tone without ever easing the standard beneath it.

The spirit. Understatement, heritage and restraint. The oldest wealth rarely announces itself; it is signalled by what is quiet, worn-in and inherited rather than by what is new and loud.

What it means for your service.

  • Discretion and formality are the default. Protocol, titles where they apply, and correct etiquette matter, and are noticed when absent.
  • Patina over polish. The family treasures the aged silver, the worn leather, the inherited piece. You never over-restore an heirloom, and you never mistake the understated for the unimportant.
  • Restraint in language. The loud superlative, the word "iconic", the visible price, all read as new money. The register is quiet.
  • Continuity. Long-standing suppliers, traditions, the way things have always been done. You honour the house's memory before you improve it.

The reflex. Quiet, precise, correct. Let nothing you do be loud, and treat the heritage of the house as something to protect rather than modernise.

The spirit. Diverse and not one culture, but with recurring themes of hierarchy, face, family and meticulous respect. A Japanese household and a Chinese one differ greatly; learn the specific family, always.

What it means for your service.

  • Face and respect. Never cause anyone to lose face, especially in front of others. Corrections and difficulties are handled privately and gently.
  • Hierarchy and the elders. Seniority is honoured carefully, in seating, in address, in the order of service.
  • Gift etiquette is precise. How a gift is wrapped, presented (often with both hands) and received matters; certain numbers and colours carry meaning. Learn them before an occasion, not during it.
  • Japanese omotenashi: a philosophy of anticipatory, selfless hospitality that expects the need to be met before it is spoken, and the effort to be invisible. It is, in many ways, the purest expression of this entire craft.
  • Family and business are often deeply intertwined; discretion spans both.

The reflex. Observe closely, honour seniority, protect everyone's face, and treat the details of ritual, the gift, the greeting, the order, as things worth getting exactly right.

Beneath every culture, the deepest codes are the same. Discretion is prized everywhere. Anticipation is valued everywhere. Respect, warmth and reliability translate into every language. A family from any corner of the world wants the same thing at heart: to feel that their private life is safe in your hands, arranged with care, and never remarked upon.

So learn the patterns, prepare for the family, and then do the one thing that works in every house on earth: read the person in front of you, and serve them as they wish to be served, not as a book, not even this one, told you they would.
